headline: Featured in Horticulture Magazine
subhead: A national spotlight on the tropical fruit trees at Palma Sola
blurb: The park's tropical fruit collection caught the eye of Horticulture Magazine.
intro: Palma Sola Botanical Park is featured in the July 2025 issue of Horticulture Magazine — one of the oldest and most respected gardening publications in the country.||The article, written by Greg Coppa, highlights the Manatee Rare Fruit Council's tropical fruit tree collection growing right here in the park. The piece showcases the remarkable variety of edible tropical species thriving in our ten acres, with photography that captures what makes this place special.
image1_caption: Fruit of a dragon fruit from the Rare Fruit Garden
aside: The Manatee Rare Fruit Council has been a longtime partner of the park, maintaining and expanding the tropical fruit collection that visitors walk through every day. Their work is a big part of what makes Palma Sola more than just a pretty garden — it's a living library of edible plants that actually grow in our climate.
body2: This is the kind of recognition that puts a small community park on the national map. We're grateful to Greg Coppa for the beautiful writing, to the Manatee Rare Fruit Council for giving him something worth writing about, and to Horticulture Magazine for telling the story.

headline: Palma Sola on Bay News 9
subhead: "Florida on a Tankful" visits the park — 33 years after neighbors saved it
blurb: Bay News 9 featured Palma Sola in their Florida on a Tankful series.
intro: Bay News 9's "Florida on a Tankful" came to Palma Sola Botanical Park — and the segment is a beautiful reminder of what this place is and how it got here.||The piece follows Executive Director Beverly Burdette through the park, from the tropical fruit trees to the wildlife on the pond, telling the story of how local citizens rallied in 1990 to save this ten-acre site from being sold as surplus land. Thirty-three years later, it's a thriving botanical park, free and open every day.
image1_caption: Click link below to watch Beverly Burdette walk with Bay News 9 through the park she's spent years helping grow.
aside: "Former tree nursery shines as botanical park in Bradenton." That's the headline Bay News 9 gave it — and we couldn't have said it better ourselves.
body2: If you missed the broadcast, the full segment is worth the watch. It captures the birds, the plants, the volunteers, and the spirit of a park that exists because a community decided it mattered.

headline: A BIG deck, thanks to the Bishop Parker Foundation
subhead: A generous grant has given the park a beautiful, lasting place to gather.
blurb: A generous grant rebuilt the park's gathering deck in lasting composite.
intro:
Some of the best things at Palma Sola happen because someone in our community decides to give. This is one of them.

Thanks to a generous grant from the Bishop Parker Foundation, the park's gathering deck has been completely rebuilt. The old boards have been replaced with durable composite decking — the kind that shrugs off Florida sun and summer rain, stays cooler underfoot, and will hold up beautifully for years without the constant upkeep that wood demands.

If you've been to a wedding here, taken a class in the shade, or just stood at the rail watching an anhinga dry its wings over the pond, you know this spot. It's one of the places the park feels most like a gathering place — and now it's ready for many more years of them.

Thanks to a generous grant from the Bishop Parker Foundation, the park's gathering deck has been completely rebuilt. The old boards have been replaced with durable composite decking — the kind that shrugs off Florida sun and summer rain, stays cooler underfoot, and will hold up beautifully for years without the constant upkeep that wood demands.
image1_caption: Brand new and built to last — the Bishop Parker deck, ready for years of community gatherings.
aside: Thank you, Bishop Parker Foundation. Gifts like this are what keep ten acres of botanical park open, free, and growing for everyone in Manatee County. We're grateful — and we can't wait to welcome you out to see it.
body2: Every event hosted on this deck helps support the park that stays free to the public, 365 days a year. Come see what's new — and stay a while.

headline: New Tables, New Chairs, New Possibilities
subhead: A $20,000 Selby Foundation grant outfits the Galleria for everything ahead
blurb: The Selby Foundation just gave our Galleria a serious upgrade — new tables, chairs, and dollies for every event to come.
intro: Thanks to a generous $20,000 grant from the William G. & Marie Selby Foundation, the Galleria at Palma Sola Botanical Park just got a complete furniture upgrade — and it changes what we can do in that space.||New tables and chairs now fill the Galleria with a full complement for training sessions, classes, community dinners, and private events. And yes, we got dollies too — so setup and teardown no longer require a small army.
aside: The Selby Foundation has a long history of investing in the organizations that make Manatee and Sarasota counties a better place to live. This grant is a perfect example — practical, lasting, and felt every time someone walks into the Galleria for an event.
body2: If you've attended a class or rented the Galleria for a wedding or dinner, you know the old furniture had done its time. These new pieces are commercial-grade and built to last — which means every event from here forward starts a little better. The grant covers it all: tables, seating, and the rolling stock to move it.||We're grateful to the Selby Foundation for seeing the value in a community park that runs on generosity and volunteer muscle. This one's going to get used.
